The [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") currently has 650 parliamentary [constituencies](/wiki/United_Kingdom_constituencies "United Kingdom constituencies") across the constituent countries ([England](/wiki/England "England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland")), each electing a single [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") by the [plurality](/wiki/Plurality_voting "Plurality voting") ([first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post")) voting system, ordinarily every five years. [Voting](/wiki/Voting "Voting") last took place in all 650 of those constituencies at the [United Kingdom general election on 4 July 2024](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").

The number of seats rose from 646 to 650 at the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election") after proposals made by the [boundary commissions for England, Wales, and Northern Ireland](/wiki/Boundary_commissions_(United_Kingdom) "Boundary commissions (United Kingdom)") (the [Fifth Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/Fifth_Periodic_Review_of_Westminster_constituencies "Fifth Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies")) were adopted through [statutory instruments](/wiki/Statutory_instruments "Statutory instruments").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Constituencies in Scotland remained unchanged, as the [Boundary Commission for Scotland](/wiki/Boundary_Commission_for_Scotland "Boundary Commission for Scotland") had completed a review just before the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), which had resulted in a reduction of 13 seats.

[Primary legislation](/wiki/Primary_legislation "Primary legislation") provides for the independence of the boundary commissions for each of the four parts of the UK, the number of seats for each of the countries, permissible factors to use in departing from any old boundaries, and a strong duty to consult. The Fifth Review was governed by the [Parliamentary Constituencies Act of 1986](/wiki/Parliamentary_Constituencies_Act_of_1986 "Parliamentary Constituencies Act of 1986"). Under the [Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011](/wiki/Parliamentary_Voting_System_and_Constituencies_Act_2011 "Parliamentary Voting System and Constituencies Act 2011"), as amended by the [Parliamentary Constituencies Act 2020](/wiki/2023_Periodic_Review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 Periodic Review of Westminster constituencies"), the number of MPs is now fixed at 650. The [Sainte-Laguë formula method](/wiki/Sainte-Lagu%C3%AB_method "Sainte-Laguë method") is used to form groups of seats split between the four parts of the United Kingdom and the English regions (as defined by the [NUTS 1 statistical regions of England](/wiki/NUTS_1_statistical_regions_of_England "NUTS 1 statistical regions of England")).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

As of 2023, every recommended constituency must have an electorate as at 2 March 2020 that is no smaller than 69,724 and no larger than 77,062.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The exceptions to this rule are five 'protected' constituencies for island areas: [Orkney and Shetland](/wiki/Orkney_and_Shetland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Orkney and Shetland (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Na h-Eileanan an Iar](/wiki/Na_h-Eileanan_an_Iar_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Na h-Eileanan an Iar (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Ynys Mon](/wiki/Ynys_M%C3%B4n_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Ynys Môn (UK Parliament constituency)"), and two constituencies on the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight").[[9]](#cite_note-9) These consequently have smaller electorates than the lower limit for other constituencies.

## Recent boundary reviews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Recent boundary reviews")]

Following the abandonment of the [Sixth Periodic Review](/wiki/2013_periodic_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2013 periodic review of Westminster constituencies") (the 2018 review), the [Boundary Commissions](/wiki/Boundary_commissions_(United_Kingdom) "Boundary commissions (United Kingdom)") formally launched the 2023 Review on 5 January 2021 and published their final proposals on 28 June 2023.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

*See [2023 review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 review of Westminster constituencies") and [List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies (2024–present) by region](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies_(2024%E2%80%93present)_by_region "List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies (2024–present) by region") for further details.*

### Summary of main boundary changes for the 2024 election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Summary of main boundary changes for the 2024 election")]

* [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") – lost 2 seats
* [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") – lost 8 seats
* [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") – no extra or fewer seats allocated
* [England](/wiki/England "England") – gained 10 seats
